authorship_evidence: DjanGoat is a Contrast Security OSS project created as a deliberately vulnerable Django application (port of RailsGoat). Commit history dates to 2017, well before LLM era. Multiple human contributors with consistent style.

evidence.description: SQL injection via string formatting in raw query. user_id_form from POST data is interpolated directly into SQL: User.objects.raw('SELECT * FROM %s WHERE user_id=\'%s\' ORDER BY is_admin=\'0\'' % (table_name, user_id_form)). An attacker can inject arbitrary SQL through the user_id POST parameter.

SQL injection via string formatting in raw query. Both ip and col parameters are interpolated directly into SQL: 'SELECT %s FROM %s WHERE ip_address=\'%s\' ORDER BY id DESC' % (col, table_name, ip). The ip parameter comes from request.GET['ip'] in admin_analytics view, and the col parameter is constructed from GET parameter keys.

Remote code execution via insecure deserialization. User-controlled POST parameter 'user' is base64-decoded and passed to pickle.loads(): user = pickle.loads(base64.b64decode(encoded_user)). An attacker can craft a malicious pickle payload to execute arbitrary code on the server.

Remote code execution via eval() on user input. The 'class' GET parameter is passed directly to eval(): model = eval(classname). An attacker can execute arbitrary Python code by passing malicious input as the class parameter.
Remote code execution via eval() on user input. The 'class' GET parameter is passed directly to eval(): model = eval(classname). Same vulnerability as api_index but in a different endpoint (api_id).

OS command injection via os.system() with unsanitized file name. The uploaded file name (orig_file.name) is interpolated into a shell command: os.system('cp %s %s' % (full_file_name, bak_file_path)). An attacker can inject shell commands through a crafted filename.

Arbitrary file read via path traversal. The 'name' GET parameter is concatenated directly to BASE_DIR without sanitization: path = BASE_DIR + r'/' + request.GET.get('name', ''). An attacker can use '../' sequences to read any file on the filesystem.

Open redirect via user-controlled 'path' POST parameter. The path from request.POST['path'] is used directly in HttpResponseRedirect(path) without validation, allowing an attacker to redirect authenticated users to a malicious site.

Hardcoded Django SECRET_KEY in settings.py: '+f0x#go-9e-qd(mq0pdn9ma*ls!h+4uy2hcofnep!o0=ow=ewn'. This secret is used for cryptographic signing (sessions, CSRF tokens, etc.). Exposure allows session forgery and CSRF bypass.
Hardcoded encryption key: KEY = b'12345678910111212345678910111212'. This key is used for AES encryption of sensitive data (bank account numbers, SSNs). The key is trivially predictable.
Hardcoded API token salt: ACCESS_TOKEN_SALT = 'S4828341189aefiasd#ASDF'. This salt is used for generating API access tokens. Exposure allows token forgery to access the API as any user.

Hardcoded user credentials in seed data including admin password 'admin1234' (line 16) and other user passwords ('yankeessuck', 'alohaowasp', 'motocross1445', 'citrusblend'). These are default credentials that would be present in any deployment using the seed command.

IDOR leading to sensitive data exposure. The user_id URL parameter is used directly to fetch user data (User.objects.get(id=user_id)) without verifying it matches the authenticated user. Any authenticated user can view another user's work info including SSN and salary by changing the user_id in the URL.

Weak password hashing using MD5. Passwords are hashed with hashlib.md5() which is cryptographically broken for password storage. MD5 is fast and susceptible to rainbow table and brute force attacks. Should use bcrypt, scrypt, or argon2.

Full unencrypted SSN exposed in HTML source. The SSN is rendered as {{ work_info.SSN }} in the HTML. Although JavaScript on lines 47-48 masks it visually with regex replacement, the full SSN is present in the page source and accessible to anyone who views source or disables JavaScript.

Username enumeration via differential error messages. When email is not found, the message is 'Email incorrect!' (line 53). When the email exists but password is wrong, the message is 'Password incorrect!' (line 56). This allows attackers to enumerate valid email addresses.

Username enumeration via password reset. When the email exists, the response is 'An email was sent to reset your password!' (line 20). When it doesn't exist, the response is 'We do not have the email in our system' (line 24). This allows attackers to enumerate valid email addresses.

Missing authorization check on admin dashboard. The @user_is_authenticated decorator only checks if a valid auth_token cookie exists, but does not verify is_admin=True. Any authenticated non-admin user can access admin functionality by navigating to /admin/<id>/dashboard/.

Missing admin authorization on user update endpoint. Any authenticated user can update any user's data including setting is_admin flag via POST to /admin/<id>/update/. The User.objects.filter().update(**data) on line 66 directly applies all POST parameters, allowing privilege escalation.
Missing admin authorization on user deletion. Any authenticated user can delete any user by sending a DELETE request to /admin/<id>/delete/. The decorator only checks authentication, not admin role.

IDOR on message access. The message is fetched by message_id (Message.objects.get(pk=message_id)) without verifying the authenticated user is the message's sender or receiver. Any authenticated user can read or delete any user's messages by changing the message_id in the URL.

Mass assignment vulnerability. All POST parameters are passed directly to User.objects.filter().update(**data) without filtering allowed fields. An attacker can set arbitrary model fields including is_admin, auth_token, user_id, etc.

Django DEBUG mode is enabled (DEBUG = True). In production, this exposes detailed stack traces, settings, SQL queries, and local variables to any user who triggers an error, leaking sensitive information about the application internals.
ALLOWED_HOSTS set to ['*'] which accepts requests with any Host header. This can enable host header injection attacks, cache poisoning, and password reset poisoning.

Predictable password reset token using MD5. Token is generated as user_id + '-' + MD5(email). Since user_id and email are often known or guessable, an attacker can forge valid password reset tokens and take over any account without needing access to the reset email.

No authentication required for password reset endpoint. The reset_password_handler and reset_password functions have no authentication decorator. Combined with the predictable token (djangoat-025) and pickle deserialization (djangoat-003), this enables unauthenticated account takeover and RCE.

Stored XSS via mark_safe() on user-controlled data. The safe_name() method calls mark_safe(self.first_name) which marks the user's first name as safe HTML, bypassing Django's auto-escaping. If a user registers with a first_name containing JavaScript (e.g., <script>alert(1)</script>), it will be rendered unescaped wherever safe_name is used.

Broken API authentication via weak regex-based token validation. The comment on line 41 explicitly states 'This is purposely vulnerable'. The regex '(.*?)-(.*)' is overly permissive - group(1) captures everything before the first dash. Combined with the hardcoded ACCESS_TOKEN_SALT, an attacker who knows the salt can forge tokens for any user.

Authentication cookie set without Secure or HttpOnly flags. The auth_token cookie is set via response.set_cookie('auth_token', user.auth_token) without secure=True or httponly=True parameters. This makes the token accessible to JavaScript (enabling theft via XSS) and transmittable over unencrypted HTTP.

Path traversal in file upload. The uploaded file name (uploaded_file.name) is used directly in os.path.join(data_path, uploaded_file.name) without sanitization. An attacker can upload files to arbitrary locations by using '../' in the filename.

IDOR on pay record deletion. Pay.objects.get(id=pay_id).delete() deletes any pay record by ID without verifying the authenticated user owns that record. Any authenticated user can delete another user's direct deposit information.

Weak auth token generation using MD5 with limited randomness. Token is generated as MD5(email + random(1,1000000)). The random space is only 1 million values, and MD5 is fast, making brute-force feasible. An attacker who knows a user's email can generate all possible tokens quickly.

DOM-based XSS via document.write() on signup page. The template reads the URL hash fragment (location.hash), decodes it with decodeURIComponent(), and passes it directly to document.write() without sanitization. An attacker can craft a URL like /signup#lang=<script>alert(document.cookie)</script> to execute arbitrary JavaScript in the victim's browser, stealing the auth_token cookie. This is a distinct client-side XSS not covered by the existing stored XSS finding (djangoat-027 covers mark_safe in user.py).

Broken CSRF protection due to template tag typo. The CSRF token tag is malformed: '{ % crsf_token % }' instead of '{% csrf_token %}'. The extra spaces inside braces and the typo 'crsf' (instead of 'csrf') mean the token is never rendered, leaving the admin user update/delete form without CSRF protection. An attacker can craft a page that auto-submits requests to modify or delete users when an authenticated admin visits it.

XSS via unquoted HTML attribute values in admin modal template. The template renders user fields in HTML attributes without quotes (e.g., value={{user.email}}). Even with Django's autoescaping, unquoted attribute values allow attribute injection — an attacker who controls the email field can inject additional HTML attributes like 'test onfocus=alert(1) autofocus' to execute JavaScript in the admin's browser context.

XSS via Django template variable rendered inside JavaScript string literal. The performance template renders review.date_for_chart inside a JS string using Django template tags without the |escapejs filter. Django's auto-escaping only escapes HTML entities (<, >, &, etc.) but not JavaScript string delimiters (single quotes, backslashes). If an attacker controls performance review data, they can inject JavaScript that executes in other users' browsers when viewing performance pages.

Use of deprecated PyCrypto library with AES-CBC mode for encrypting sensitive data (SSNs, bank account numbers). PyCrypto is unmaintained and has known vulnerabilities (e.g., heap buffer overflow CVE-2013-7459). AES-CBC without message authentication (no HMAC/AEAD) is vulnerable to padding oracle and bit-flipping attacks. This is distinct from djangoat-010 which covers the hardcoded encryption key.

Static IV reuse with AES-CBC mode. The get_iv() method retrieves a static IV from the KeyManagement model for each user, reusing it for every encryption operation. In AES-CBC, reusing the same key+IV combination for different plaintexts leaks information about plaintext relationships and undermines semantic security. This is distinct from the hardcoded key issue (djangoat-010).

Padding oracle vulnerability via custom PKCS7 padding removal without authenticated encryption. The unpad method performs s[:-ord(s[len(s)-1:])] without integrity verification. Combined with AES-CBC mode, this creates a classic padding oracle where an attacker can manipulate ciphertext and observe differential error behavior to decrypt sensitive data (SSNs, bank account numbers) byte-by-byte without the key.

Session fixation via static auth_token that is never regenerated upon login. The login handler authenticates the user and sets the auth_token cookie, but the token is the same static value stored in the database — never rotated on authentication. If an attacker can preset or predict a user's token (feasible given djangoat-032's weak generation), they retain access post-authentication. This is distinct from djangoat-029 (missing cookie flags) and djangoat-032 (weak token entropy).

Password reset token has no expiration. The is_valid_token function only checks that the MD5 hash matches the user's email — there is no timestamp or expiration check. Tokens remain valid indefinitely. A token intercepted via email logs, referrer headers, or network interception can be used at any time in the future for account takeover. This is distinct from djangoat-025 which covers token predictability.

Hardcoded database credentials in Docker settings: username 'root' with password 'password'. These are committed to source control and use the database superuser account with a trivially guessable password. This is a separate settings file (docker_settings.py) from the secrets in pygoat/settings.py already covered by djangoat-009/010/011, and covers database credentials specifically.

Message spoofing via unvalidated creator_id in POST data. The user_messages POST handler takes creator_id from request.POST['creator_id'] without verifying it matches the authenticated user. An attacker can send messages impersonating any user by setting creator_id to another user's ID. This is distinct from djangoat-021 which covers IDOR on message READ/DELETE access — this finding is about message CREATION with forged sender identity.

No authentication on mobile API endpoints. The api_index mobile endpoint has no authentication or authorization checks — any unauthenticated user can access it. Even without the eval() vulnerability (djangoat-004), if the class parameter is 'User', all user records including PII are returned without authentication. This is a distinct vulnerability (missing authentication) from the code injection via eval() already in ground truth.

Mass assignment / privilege escalation in user self-update flow. The user_view function at line 89 uses User.objects.filter(pk=user.pk).update(**update) where the update dict comes from validate_update_form. If validate_update_form does not explicitly exclude privileged fields, any authenticated user can escalate to admin by including is_admin=True in the POST request. This is distinct from djangoat-022 which covers mass assignment in the ADMIN update endpoint (admin/views.py:66) — this finding affects the REGULAR USER self-update endpoint, meaning no admin access is needed to exploit it.

Hardcoded plaintext credentials for multiple user accounts (including admin) exposed in a public-facing template. The not_logged_in.html template serves these credentials to unauthenticated visitors in the HTML source. This is distinct from djangoat-012 (seed.py credentials requiring file system access) — these credentials are served directly over HTTP to any visitor without authentication, making them immediately exploitable for account takeover including the admin account.

Mass data exposure via admin API endpoint that serializes ALL User objects with ALL fields using serializers.serialize('json', User.objects.all()). No field-level filtering restricts which attributes are returned, exposing passwords/hashes, auth tokens, PII, and internal state for every user. Combined with the broken token authentication (djangoat-028), any attacker who forges an admin token gets access to all user data. This is distinct from djangoat-028 which covers the broken auth mechanism — this finding covers the data over-exposure at the endpoint itself.

DOM-based XSS via jQuery .html() method in decryptDirectDeposit function. The server response from the decrypt endpoint is inserted directly into the DOM using .html() without sanitization. If the decrypted bank account data contains user-controlled content (e.g., account holder name or memo), an attacker could store a JavaScript payload that executes when the data is decrypted and rendered. Additionally, the jQuery selector uses string concatenation with user input: $('.accNum:contains(\'' + input + '\')'), enabling selector injection.

Reflected XSS via user_id_form from POST data rendered in HttpResponse without HTML escaping. At line 80, HttpResponse('User ' + str(user_id_form) + ' NOT FOUND') reflects the user-supplied POST parameter directly in the response body. An attacker can inject HTML/JavaScript via the user_id POST field. This is distinct from djangoat-001 which covers SQL injection on the same input — this finding covers the separate XSS vulnerability when the SQL query returns no results.

Reflected XSS via user_id URL path parameter rendered in HttpResponse without HTML escaping. At line 101, HttpResponse('User ' + str(user_id) + ' ' + str(request.method)) reflects the URL parameter directly in the response body. If the Django URL pattern does not enforce integer-only matching for user_id, an attacker can inject HTML/JavaScript via the URL path. Similar unescaped reflections of user_id also exist at lines 55 and 69 in the same file.

NOT VULNERABLE. The parse_field() method validates the field parameter against an allowlist of valid_fields = ['ip_address', 'referrer', 'user_agent']. If the field is not in the list, it returns '1'. This prevents SQL injection through field name manipulation.

NOT VULNERABLE. The selected_id is cast to int() before being passed to User.objects.get(user_id=int(selected_id)). The Django ORM parameterizes the query. The int() cast prevents any SQL injection via the URL parameter.
NOT VULNERABLE. User.objects.filter(email=request.POST.get('email', '')) uses Django's ORM with keyword argument filtering, which parameterizes the query. User input is not interpolated into raw SQL.

NOT VULNERABLE. {{ message.message }} uses Django's default auto-escaping which escapes <, >, &, ', and " characters. The message content is properly escaped before rendering in the template. The |safe filter is not used.

PARTIALLY MITIGATED but still vulnerable (see djangoat-030). The data_path uses os.path.join(settings.MEDIA_ROOT, 'data') which is a fixed base path, but the uploaded_file.name on line 33 is not sanitized. Note: os.path.join with an absolute second argument would replace the first, but Django's UploadedFile.name is typically relative. The real risk is in the filename containing traversal sequences. Documenting as FP for the data_path construction itself.

NOT a real credential exposure. The reviewer_data tuple contains test/fixture data used for build_benefits_data() including 'auth_token' as a literal string value. This is test data internal to the application's data seeding, not a real authentication credential that grants access.
